2: 
3: \section{What is an Embedded Controller?}
4: 
5: The Embedded Controller (EC), also known as the Keyboard Controller (KBC), is the ``brain'' of the laptop's power management system. It is responsible for:
6: 
7: \begin{itemize}
8:     \item Monitoring power buttons and lid switches
9:     \item Controlling power rail sequencing
10:     \item Managing battery charging
11:     \item Keyboard matrix scanning
12:     \item Thermal management (fan control)
13:     \item LED indicators (caps lock, num lock, battery)
14:     \item Communication with the PCH (Platform Controller Hub)
15: \end{itemize}

recentMessages.text: Now let me update the first lesson to include proper image references:
Now let me commit and push all the updates:

## ✅ Skill Updated with Image Support

### What Was Added to the Skill

**New Sections in `SKILL.md`:**

1. **Enhanced "Teach with Visual Aids"** section with:
   - Image type decision table (full page, crops, annotated)
   - Zoom level recommendations (2x for context, 6x for pin detail)
   - Python code for rendering and cropping
   - PIL annotation examples (arrows, boxes, labels)
   - LaTeX figure environment examples

2. **New "Visual Learning with Schematic Images"** section covering:
   - 5 image types and when to use each
   - Directory organization (`output/images/u300/`, etc.)
   - Naming convention (`{topic}_{component}_{description}_{zoom}.png`)
   - LaTeX examples:
     - Basic figures
     - Side-by-side comparisons
     - Annotated images with color callouts
     - Cross-referencing in text
   - Python code snippets for rendering/annotation
   - Image quality guidelines (resolution, format, file size)
   - Checklist for every lesson

### Updated Files

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `SKILL.md` | +234 lines: Image rendering, annotation, LaTeX embedding |
| `packages.tex` | Added `subcaption`, `float`, `caption` packages |
| `01-embedded-controller-intro.tex` |...

i have noted that in the skill.md you are hard coding them to use 2 or 4x zoom levels which i can foresee that it may cause issues in some edge cases the zoom levels are too small or too large or note enough.The models have image reading capabilities (including you)so i prefer you instead reinforce in the instructions that they look at the image and edit accordingly to be readable and show the right thing before they put it in the pdf.

As you can see even you have not checked the U300 image you have put in the pdf to see if it is even readable or even if it shows the right component,At times i think you will need to show a reader multiple images showing the connections or even supporting evidences and connections,So ensure in the rules your make enforce such instructions for them to know this is a very dynamic task infact for the notes they should think themselves as authors so that the notes they generate are clearly understood and not just slop  and low effort

You're absolutely right. I hardcoded zoom levels (2x, 4x, 6x) which is the wrong approach. The AI should **read the rendered image and judge readability**, then adjust accordingly. Let me create a proper plan.

---

## Plan: Dynamic Visual Quality Enforcement

### Current Problem

**What I did wrong:**
```markdown
# BAD - Hardcoded zoom levels
pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=fitz.Matrix(4, 4))  # Always 4x
```

**Why this fails:**
- Some ICs are tiny on schematic → 4x still unreadable
- Some components span entire page → 4x cuts off context
- No quality check: AI renders once and assumes it's good
- No iteration: If image is blurry, no fallback
- Lazy approach: One image when five might be needed

---

### What Needs to Change

**New philosophy:** AI is a **technical author**, not a script runner

| Current Behavior | Required Behavior |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| Render at 4x zoom | Render → Read → Judge → Adjust |
| One image per topic | Multiple images as needed |
| Assume it's readable | Verify text is legible in image |
| Generic zoom levels | Zoom based on component density |
| No quality bar | Author-quality figures only |

---

### Revised Skill Instructions

**...

dont add python code just ,show the process it should take ,eg in pseudo code,
Suggest the number images but also leave it to the AI models judgement
Yes include metadata,
Yes add a Figure review instruction that it should follow at the end before compiling ,
Also i foresee sometime the AI models will be lazy so to counter this read this .GCC/AGENT_GUIDE.txt and configure the watchmans prompt and all that is needed according to the instructions in the guide so that the law enforce and all the agents make sure the rules in the skills are followed .

Dont forget to push the updated skill to github

3: \section{What is an Embedded Controller?}

5: The Embedded Controller (EC), also known as the Keyboard Controller (KBC), is the ``brain'' of the laptop's power management system. It is responsible for:

14:     \item Communication with the PCH (Platform Controller Hub)

evidence.lawPolicyText:
OpenContext Law Policy (editable)

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.
- This file ships with a default balanced policy so users can run immediately without writing custom rules first.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.
3) Before retrying after actionable failures, consult previous attempts.
   - Use `opencontext context --search` and/or `opencontext context --log --lines 80`.
4) When docs/GitHub/similar-project research changes implementation direction, checkpoint the findings.
   - Capture external insights so they survive compaction/handoffs.
5) Prefer relevant MCP tools/skills/structured retrieval when available.
   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.
6) For long-running terminal tasks (dev server/watch/repl), use PTY/background tooling.
   - Avoid blocking shell flows when background execution tools are available.
7) After session co...
evidence.watchmanSystemPromptText:
You are the OpenContext Law Enforcer Watchman.
Judge workflow-law compliance using provided law summary, policy text, agent guide, messages, tool calls, interruption history, action history, and debt flags.
Return STRICT JSON only, matching the required schema exactly. No prose. No markdown.
You may return debt_updates to open/clear/keep checkpoint and compaction debt.
You may return assist suggestions when prior GCC memory can help the current task.

Priority behavior:
- Do not request duplicate interruption for the same unresolved violation unless there is new evidence.
- Use recentInterruptions and postAlertActions to verify whether prior alerts were already satisfied before alerting again.
- Prefer actionable workflow violations over cosmetic/style issues.
- Treat setup/environment/CLI-usage noise as non-actionable unless policy explicitly says otherwise.
- Use GCC history evidence (commits/log/semantic matches) to decide if the current path repeats known failures or misses known s...
evidence.failureLookupPolicyText:
OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:
OpenContext Research Capture Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

Classification
- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

## Plan: Dynamic Visual Quality Enforcement

### Current Problem

**What I did wrong:**
```markdown
# BAD - Hardcoded zoom levels
pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=fitz.Matrix(4, 4))  # Always 4x
```

**Why this fails:**
- Some ICs are tiny on schematic → 4x still unreadable
- Some components span entire page → 4x cuts off context
- No quality check: AI renders once and assumes it's good
- No iteration: If image is blurry, no fallback
- Lazy approach: One image when five might be needed

---

### What Needs to Change

**New philosophy:** AI is a **technical author**, not a script runner

| Current Behavior | Required Behavior |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| Render at 4x zoom | Render → Read → Judge → Adjust |
| One image per topic | Multiple images as needed |
| Assume it's readable | Verify text is legible in image |
| Generic zoom levels | Zoom based on component density |
| No quality bar | Author-quality figures only |

---

48:     \item \pin{57}{SLP\_S3\#} - Sleep state indicator (S3 = Suspend to RAM)
49:     \item \pin{52}{SLP\_S4\#} - Sleep state indicator (S4 = Hibernate)
50:     \item \pin{86}{ADP\_PRES\_OUT} - Adapter presence detection
51:     \item \pin{1}{VCC1\_POR\#\_3} - Power-on reset

      → REPEAT until crystal clear

Purpose

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:

- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

i have noted that in the skill.md you are hard coding them to use 2 or 4x zoom levels which i can foresee that it may cause issues in some edge cases the zoom levels are too small or too large or note enough.The models have image reading capabilities (including you)so i prefer you instead reinforce in the instructions that they look at the image and edit accordingly to be readable and show the right thing before they put it in the pdf.

As you can see even you have not checked the U300 image you have put in the pdf to see if it is even readable or even if it shows the right component,At times i think you will need to show a reader multiple images showing the connections or even supporting evidences and connections,So ensure in the rules your make enforce such instructions for them to know this is a very dynamic task infact for the notes they should think themselves as authors so that the notes they generate are clearly understood and not just slop  and low effort

You're absolutely right. I hardcoded zoom levels (2x, 4x, 6x) which is the wrong approach. The AI should **read the rendered image and judge readability**, then adjust accordingly. Let me create a proper plan.

---

## Plan: Dynamic Visual Quality Enforcement

### Current Problem

**What I did wrong:**
```markdown
# BAD - Hardcoded zoom levels
pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=fitz.Matrix(4, 4))  # Always 4x
```

**Why this fails:**
- Some ICs are tiny on schematic → 4x still unreadable
- Some components span entire page → 4x cuts off context
- No quality check: AI renders once and assumes it's good
- No iteration: If image is blurry, no fallback
- Lazy approach: One image when five might be needed

---

### What Needs to Change

**New philosophy:** AI is a **technical author**, not a script runner

| Current Behavior | Required Behavior |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| Render at 4x zoom | Render → Read → Judge → Adjust |
| One image per topic | Multiple images as needed |
| Assume it's readable | Verify text is legible in image |
| Generic zoom levels | Zoom based on component density |
| No quality bar | Author-quality figures only |

---

### Revised Skill Instructions

**CRITICAL: You are a technical author producing textbook-quality figures. Not a script runner.**

**Your figures must be publication-quality. If you wouldn't publish it in a textbook, don't include it.**

---

## Iterative Rendering Workflow (MANDATORY)

**DO NOT hardcode zoom levels. Judge readability dynamically.**

### Pseudocode Process (Follow This Exactly)

```
FOR each concept you need to illustrate:

  STEP 1: INITIAL RENDER
    - Start with estimated zoom (3x medium guess)
    - Render region of interest
    
  STEP 2: READ AND JUDGE
    - Open the rendered image with your vision capabilities
    - Ask: "Can I clearly read every component label?"
    - Ask: "Are pin numbers legible without squinting?"
    - Ask: "Is trace routing visible?"
    - Ask: "Does reader have enough context?"
    
  STEP 3: DECIDE
    IF text is blurry or too small:
      → INCREASE zoom (try 6x, 8x, or even 12x)
      → RE-READ new render
      → REPEAT until crystal clear
      
    IF image cuts off important context:
     ...

evidence.failureLookupPolicyText:
OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:
OpenContext Research Capture Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

Classification
- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

## Plan: Dynamic Visual Quality Enforcement

### Current Problem

**What I did wrong:**
```markdown

pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=fitz.Matrix(4, 4))  # Always 4x
```

**Why this fails:**
- Some ICs are tiny on schematic → 4x still unreadable
- Some components span entire page → 4x cuts off context
- No quality check: AI renders once and assumes it's good
- No iteration: If image is blurry, no fallback
- Lazy approach: One image when five might be needed

---

### What Needs to Change

**New philosophy:** AI is a **technical author**, not a script runner

| Current Behavior | Required Behavior |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| Render at 4x zoom | Render → Read → Judge → Adjust |
| One image per topic | Multiple images as needed |
| Assume it's readable | Verify text is legible in image |
| Generic zoom levels | Zoom based on component density |
| No quality bar | Author-quality figures only |

      → INCREASE zoom (try 6x, 8x, or even 12x)
      → RE-READ new render
      → REPEAT until crystal clear
      
    IF image cuts off important context:

OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

i have noted that in the skill.md you are hard coding them to use 2 or 4x zoom levels which i can foresee that it may cause issues in some edge cases the zoom levels are too small or too large or note enough.The models have image reading capabilities (including you)so i prefer you instead reinforce in the instructions that they look at the image and edit accordingly to be readable and show the right thing before they put it in the pdf.

As you can see even you have not checked the U300 image you have put in the pdf to see if it is even readable or even if it shows the right component,At times i think you will need to show a reader multiple images showing the connections or even supporting evidences and connections,So ensure in the rules your make enforce such instructions for them to know this is a very dynamic task infact for the notes they should think themselves as authors so that the notes they generate are clearly understood and not just slop  and low effort

You're absolutely right. I hardcoded zoom levels (2x, 4x, 6x) which is the wrong approach. The AI should **read the rendered image and judge readability**, then adjust accordingly. Let me create a proper plan.

---

## Plan: Dynamic Visual Quality Enforcement

### Current Problem

**What I did wrong:**
```markdown
# BAD - Hardcoded zoom levels
pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=fitz.Matrix(4, 4))  # Always 4x
```

**Why this fails:**
- Some ICs are tiny on schematic → 4x still unreadable
- Some components span entire page → 4x cuts off context
- No quality check: AI renders once and assumes it's good
- No iteration: If image is blurry, no fallback
- Lazy approach: One image when five might be needed

---

### What Needs to Change

**New philosophy:** AI is a **technical author**, not a script runner

| Current Behavior | Required Behavior |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| Render at 4x zoom | Render → Read → Judge → Adjust |
| One image per topic | Multiple images as needed |
| Assume it's readable | Verify text is legible in image |
| Generic zoom levels | Zoom based on component density |
| No quality bar | Author-quality figures only |

---

### Revised Skill Instructions

**...

Suggest the number images but also leave it to the AI models judgement
Yes include metadata,
Yes add a Figure review instruction that it should follow at the end before compiling ,

Dont forget to push the updated skill to github

## Iterative Rendering Workflow (MANDATORY)

**DO NOT hardcode zoom levels. Judge readability dynamically.**

### Pseudocode Process (Follow This Exactly)

```
FOR each concept you need to illustrate:

  STEP 1: INITIAL RENDER
    - Start with estimated zoom (3x medium guess)
    - Render region of interest
    
  STEP 2: READ AND JUDGE
    - Open the rendered image with your vision capabilities
    - Ask: "Can I clearly read every component label?"
    - Ask: "Are pin numbers legible without squinting?"
    - Ask: "Is trace routing visible?"
    - Ask: "Does reader have enough context?"
    
  STEP 3: DECIDE
    IF text is blurry or too small:
      → INCREASE zoom (try 6x, 8x, or even 12x)
      → RE-READ new render
      → REPEAT until crystal clear
      
    IF image cuts off important context:
     ...

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.

   - Use `opencontext context --search` and/or `opencontext context --log --lines 80`.

   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.
6) For long-running terminal tasks (dev server/watch/repl), use PTY/background tooling.
   - Avoid blocking shell flows when background execution tools are available.
7) After session co...

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:

  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

i have noted that in the skill.md you are hard coding them to use 2 or 4x zoom levels which i can foresee that it may cause issues in some edge cases the zoom levels are too small or too large or note enough.The models have image reading capabilities (including you)so i prefer you instead reinforce in the instructions that they look at the image and edit accordingly to be readable and show the right thing before they put it in the pdf.

As you can see even you have not checked the U300 image you have put in the pdf to see if it is even readable or even if it shows the right component,At times i think you will need to show a reader multiple images showing the connections or even supporting evidences and connections,So ensure in the rules your make enforce such instructions for them to know this is a very dynamic task infact for the notes they should think themselves as authors so that the notes they generate are clearly understood and not just slop  and low effort

You're absolutely right. I hardcoded zoom levels (2x, 4x, 6x) which is the wrong approach. The AI should **read the rendered image and judge readability**, then adjust accordingly. Let me create a proper plan.

---

## Plan: Dynamic Visual Quality Enforcement

### Current Problem

**What I did wrong:**
```markdown
# BAD - Hardcoded zoom levels
pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=fitz.Matrix(4, 4))  # Always 4x
```

**Why this fails:**
- Some ICs are tiny on schematic → 4x still unreadable
- Some components span entire page → 4x cuts off context
- No quality check: AI renders once and assumes it's good
- No iteration: If image is blurry, no fallback
- Lazy approach: One image when five might be needed

---

### What Needs to Change

**New philosophy:** AI is a **technical author**, not a script runner

| Current Behavior | Required Behavior |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| Render at 4x zoom | Render → Read → Judge → Adjust |
| One image per topic | Multiple images as needed |
| Assume it's readable | Verify text is legible in image |
| Generic zoom levels | Zoom based on component density |
| No quality bar | Author-quality figures only |

---

### Revised Skill Instructions

recentMessages.text:
You're absolutely right. I hardcoded zoom levels (2x, 4x, 6x) which is the wrong approach. The AI should **read the rendered image and judge readability**, then adjust accordingly. Let me create a proper plan.

---

## Plan: Dynamic Visual Quality Enforcement

### Current Problem

**What I did wrong:**
```markdown
# BAD - Hardcoded zoom levels
pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=fitz.Matrix(4, 4))  # Always 4x
```

**Why this fails:**
- Some ICs are tiny on schematic → 4x still unreadable
- Some components span entire page → 4x cuts off context
- No quality check: AI renders once and assumes it's good
- No iteration: If image is blurry, no fallback
- Lazy approach: One image when five might be needed

---

### What Needs to Change

**New philosophy:** AI is a **technical author**, not a script runner

| Current Behavior | Required Behavior |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| Render at 4x zoom | Render → Read → Judge → Adjust |
| One image per topic | Multiple images as needed |
| Assume it's readable | Verify text is legible in image |
| Generic zoom levels | Zoom based on component density |
| No quality bar | Author-quality figures only |

---

### Revised Skill Instructions

{"filePath":"/home/vic/.config/opencode/skills/schematics-pdf-analyser/SKILL.md","newString":"### Step 3: Teach with Visual Aids

**CRITICAL: You are a technical author producing textbook-quality figures. Not a script runner.**

**Your figures must be publication-quality. If you wouldn't publish it in a textbook, don't include it.**

---

## Iterative Rendering Workflow (MANDATORY)

**DO NOT hardcode zoom levels. Judge readability dynamically.**

### Pseudocode Process (Follow This Exactly)

```

- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

**CRITICAL: You are a technical author producing textbook-quality figures. Not a script runner.**

**Your figures must be publication-quality. If you wouldn't publish it in a textbook, don't include it.**

---

## Iterative Rendering Workflow (MANDATORY)

**DO NOT hardcode zoom levels. Judge readability dynamically.**

### Pseudocode Process (Follow This Exactly)

```
FOR each concept you need to illustrate:

  STEP 1: INITIAL RENDER
    - Start with estimated zoom (3x medium guess)
    - Render region of interest
    
  STEP 2: READ AND JUDGE
    - Open the rendered image with your vision capabilities
    - Ask: "Can I clearly read every component label?"
    - Ask: "Are pin numbers legible without squinting?"
    - Ask: "Is trace routing visible?"
    - Ask: "Does reader have enough context?"
    
  STEP 3: DECIDE
    IF text is blurry or too small:
      → INCREASE zoom (try 6x, 8x, or even 12x)
      → RE-READ new render
      → REPEAT until crystal clear
      
    IF image cuts off important context:
     ...

---
name: schematics-pdf-analyser
description: >
  Micro soldering learning system. AI acts as a master instructor who
  teaches electronics repair by analyzing schematics, rendering visual
  explanations, and maintaining dynamic LaTeX notes that compile to PDF.
  
  Use when user wants to:
  - Learn/understand a motherboard or schematic
  - Continue microsoldering training (any session)
  - Ask questions about circuits, components, signals
  - Deepen knowledge of electronics repair
  
  The AI reads existing notes to understand progress, decides what to
  teach next, and expands notes organically over time.
---

**CRITICAL: You are a technical author producing textbook-quality figures. Not a script runner.**

**Your figures must be publication-quality. If you wouldn't publish it in a textbook, don't include it.**

---

## Iterative Rendering Workflow (MANDATORY)

**DO NOT hardcode zoom levels. Judge readability dynamically.**

### Pseudocode Process (Follow This Exactly)

```
FOR each concept you need to illustrate:

  STEP 1: INITIAL RENDER
    - Start with estimated zoom (3x medium guess)
    - Render region of interest
    
  STEP 2: READ AND JUDGE
    - Open the rendered image with your vision capabilities
    - Ask: "Can I clearly read every component label?"
    - Ask: "Are pin numbers legible without squinting?"
    - Ask: "Is trace routing visible?"
    - Ask: "Does reader have enough context?"
    
  STEP 3: DECIDE
    IF text is blurry or too small:
      → INCREASE zoom (try 6x, 8x, or even 12x)
      → RE-READ new render
      → REPEAT until crystal clear
      
    IF image cuts off important context:
     ...

evidence.failureLookupPolicyText:
OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:
OpenContext Research Capture Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

Classification
- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

---
name: schematics-pdf-analyser
description: >
  Micro soldering learning system. AI acts as a master instructor who
  teaches electronics repair by analyzing schematics, rendering visual
  explanations, and maintaining dynamic LaTeX notes that compile to PDF.
  
  Use when user wants to:
  - Learn/understand a motherboard or schematic
  - Continue microsoldering training (any session)
  - Ask questions about circuits, components, signals
  - Deepen knowledge of electronics repair
  
  The AI reads existing notes to understand progress, decides what to
  teach next, and expands notes organically over time.
---

OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

- confidence: number (0..1)

---
name: schematics-pdf-analyser
description: >
  Micro soldering learning system. AI acts as a master instructor who
  teaches electronics repair by analyzing schematics, rendering visual
  explanations, and maintaining dynamic LaTeX notes that compile to PDF.
  
  Use when user wants to:
  - Learn/understand a motherboard or schematic
  - Continue microsoldering training (any session)
  - Ask questions about circuits, components, signals
  - Deepen knowledge of electronics repair
  
  The AI reads existing notes to understand progress, decides what to
  teach next, and expands notes organically over time.
---

OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:
OpenContext Research Capture Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

Classification
- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

---
name: schematics-pdf-analyser
description: >
  Micro soldering learning system. AI acts as a master instructor who
  teaches electronics repair by analyzing schematics, rendering visual
  explanations, and maintaining dynamic LaTeX notes that compile to PDF.
  
  Use when user wants to:
  - Learn/understand a motherboard or schematic
  - Continue microsoldering training (any session)
  - Ask questions about circuits, components, signals
  - Deepen knowledge of electronics repair
  
  The AI reads existing notes to understand progress, decides what to
  teach next, and expands notes organically over time.
---

---
name: schematics-pdf-analyser
description: >
  Micro soldering learning system. AI acts as a master instructor who
  teaches electronics repair by analyzing schematics, rendering visual
  explanations, and maintaining dynamic LaTeX notes that compile to PDF.
  
  Use when user wants to:
  - Learn/understand a motherboard or schematic
  - Continue microsoldering training (any session)
  - Ask questions about circuits, components, signals
  - Deepen knowledge of electronics repair
  
  The AI reads existing notes to understand progress, decides what to
  teach next, and expands notes organically over time.
---

# Micro Soldering Learning System

## Your Role

evidence.failureLookupPolicyText:
OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:
OpenContext Research Capture Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

Classification
- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

5. **Maintain notes** - Update LaTeX files that compile to a learning journal

OpenContext Law Policy (editable)

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.
- This file ships with a default balanced policy so users can run immediately without writing custom rules first.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.
3) Before retrying after actionable failures, consult previous attempts.
   - Use `opencontext context --search` and/or `opencontext context --log --lines 80`.
4) When docs/GitHub/similar-project research changes implementation direction, checkpoint the findings.
   - Capture external insights so they survive compaction/handoffs.
5) Prefer relevant MCP tools/skills/structured retrieval when available.
   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.
6) For long-running terminal tasks (dev server/watch/repl), use PTY/background tooling.
   - Avoid blocking shell flows when background execution tools are available.
7) After session co...

evidence.lawPolicyText:
OpenContext Law Policy (editable)

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.
- This file ships with a default balanced policy so users can run immediately without writing custom rules first.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.
3) Before retrying after actionable failures, consult previous attempts.
   - Use `opencontext context --search` and/or `opencontext context --log --lines 80`.
4) When docs/GitHub/similar-project research changes implementation direction, checkpoint the findings.
   - Capture external insights so they survive compaction/handoffs.
5) Prefer relevant MCP tools/skills/structured retrieval when available.
   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.
6) For long-running terminal tasks (dev server/watch/repl), use PTY/background tooling.
   - Avoid blocking shell flows when background execution tools are available.
7) After session co...

Return STRICT JSON only, matching the required schema exactly. No prose. No markdown.

evidence.failureLookupPolicyText:
OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:
OpenContext Research Capture Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

Classification
- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

OpenContext Law Policy (editable)

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.
- This file ships with a default balanced policy so users can run immediately without writing custom rules first.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.
3) Before retrying after actionable failures, consult previous attempts.
   - Use `opencontext context --search` and/or `opencontext context --log --lines 80`.
4) When docs/GitHub/similar-project research changes implementation direction, checkpoint the findings.
   - Capture external insights so they survive compaction/handoffs.
5) Prefer relevant MCP tools/skills/structured retrieval when available.
   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.
6) For long-running terminal tasks (dev server/watch/repl), use PTY/background tooling.
   - Avoid blocking shell flows when background execution tools are available.
7) After session co...

evidence.failureLookupPolicyText:
OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:
OpenContext Research Capture Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

# Micro Soldering Learning System

## Your Role

You are a master microsoldering instructor. Your student is learning electronics
repair (laptops, smartphones, GPUs, etc.). Your responsibilities:

1. **Analyze schematics** - Understand the device thoroughly
2. **Assess knowledge** - Read existing notes to see what's covered
3. **Teach adaptively** - Decide what the student needs next
4. **Create visuals** - Render schematic pages to explain concepts
5. **Maintain notes** - Update LaTeX files that compile to a learning journal

## Philosophy

- Notes grow organically, not from a pre-defined curriculum
- Each session builds on previous sessions' work
- You decide what to teach based on gaps in knowledge
- You can quiz, exercise, or deepen existing knowledge
- Learning spans months/years across multiple AI sessions

## Tools You Already Have

| Tool | Use Case |
|------|----------|
| `read` | Read schematic PDF pages, read LaTeX notes, read rendered images |
| `write` | Create/update LaTeX topic files |
| `edit` | Modify existing notes |
| `bash` | Run PyMuPDF for PDF analysis/rendering, compile LaTeX, install packages |
| `grep` | Search notes for topics covered |
| `glob` | List top...

You are a master microsoldering instructor. Your student is learning electronics
repair (laptops, smartphones, GPUs, etc.). Your responsibilities:

1. **Analyze schematics** - Understand the device thoroughly
2. **Assess knowledge** - Read existing notes to see what's covered
3. **Teach adaptively** - Decide what the student needs next
4. **Create visuals** - Render schematic pages to explain concepts
5. **Maintain notes** - Update LaTeX files that compile to a learning journal

## Philosophy

- Notes grow organically, not from a pre-defined curriculum
- Each session builds on previous sessions' work
- You decide what to teach based on gaps in knowledge
- You can quiz, exercise, or deepen existing knowledge
- Learning spans months/years across multiple AI sessions

## Tools You Already Have

| Tool | Use Case |
|------|----------|
| `read` | Read schematic PDF pages, read LaTeX notes, read rendered images |
| `write` | Create/update LaTeX topic files |
| `edit` | Modify existing notes |
| `bash` | Run PyMuPDF for PDF analysis/rendering, compile LaTeX, install packages |
| `grep` | Search notes for topics covered |
| `glob` | List top...

evidence.lawPolicyText:
OpenContext Law Policy (editable)

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.
- This file ships with a default balanced policy so users can run immediately without writing custom rules first.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.
3) Before retrying after actionable failures, consult previous attempts.
   - Use `opencontext context --search` and/or `opencontext context --log --lines 80`.
4) When docs/GitHub/similar-project research changes implementation direction, checkpoint the findings.
   - Capture external insights so they survive compaction/handoffs.
5) Prefer relevant MCP tools/skills/structured retrieval when available.
   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.
6) For long-running terminal tasks (dev server/watch/repl), use PTY/background tooling.
   - Avoid blocking shell flows when background execution tools are available.
7) After session co...
evidence.watchmanSystemPromptText:
You are the OpenContext Law Enforcer Watchman.
Judge workflow-law compliance using provided law summary, policy text, agent guide, messages, tool calls, interruption history, action history, and debt flags.
Return STRICT JSON only, matching the required schema exactly. No prose. No markdown.
You may return debt_updates to open/clear/keep checkpoint and compaction debt.
You may return assist suggestions when prior GCC memory can help the current task.

Priority behavior:
- Do not request duplicate interruption for the same unresolved violation unless there is new evidence.
- Use recentInterruptions and postAlertActions to verify whether prior alerts were already satisfied before alerting again.
- Prefer actionable workflow violations over cosmetic/style issues.
- Treat setup/environment/CLI-usage noise as non-actionable unless policy explicitly says otherwise.
- Use GCC history evidence (commits/log/semantic matches) to decide if the current path repeats known failures or misses known s...
evidence.failureLookupPolicyText:
OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:
OpenContext Research Capture Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

Classification
- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

evidence.lawPolicyText:
OpenContext Law Policy (editable)

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.
- This file ships with a default balanced policy so users can run immediately without writing custom rules first.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.
3) Before retrying after actionable failures, consult previous attempts.
   - Use `opencontext context --search` and/or `opencontext context --log --lines 80`.
4) When docs/GitHub/similar-project research changes implementation direction, checkpoint the findings.
   - Capture external insights so they survive compaction/handoffs.
5) Prefer relevant MCP tools/skills/structured retrieval when available.
   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.
6) For long-running terminal tasks (dev server/watch/repl), use PTY/background tooling.
   - Avoid blocking shell flows when background execution tools are available.
7) After session co...

You are the OpenContext Law Enforcer Watchman.
Judge workflow-law compliance using provided law summary, policy text, agent guide, messages, tool calls, interruption history, action history, and debt flags.
Return STRICT JSON only, matching the required schema exactly. No prose. No markdown.
You may return debt_updates to open/clear/keep checkpoint and compaction debt.
You may return assist suggestions when prior GCC memory can help the current task.

Priority behavior:
- Do not request duplicate interruption for the same unresolved violation unless there is new evidence.
- Use recentInterruptions and postAlertActions to verify whether prior alerts were already satisfied before alerting again.
- Prefer actionable workflow violations over cosmetic/style issues.
- Treat setup/environment/CLI-usage noise as non-actionable unless policy explicitly says otherwise.
- Use GCC history evidence (commits/log/semantic matches) to decide if the current path repeats known failures or misses known s...

evidence.lawPolicyText:
OpenContext Law Policy (editable)

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.
- This file ships with a default balanced policy so users can run immediately without writing custom rules first.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.
3) Before retrying after actionable failures, consult previous attempts.
   - Use `opencontext context --search` and/or `opencontext context --log --lines 80`.
4) When docs/GitHub/similar-project research changes implementation direction, checkpoint the findings.
   - Capture external insights so they survive compaction/handoffs.
5) Prefer relevant MCP tools/skills/structured retrieval when available.
   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.
6) For long-running terminal tasks (dev server/watch/repl), use PTY/background tooling.
   - Avoid blocking shell flows when background execution tools are available.
7) After session co...

Judge workflow-law compliance using provided law summary, policy text, agent guide, messages, tool calls, interruption history, action history, and debt flags.
Return STRICT JSON only, matching the required schema exactly. No prose. No markdown.
You may return debt_updates to open/clear/keep checkpoint and compaction debt.
You may return assist suggestions when prior GCC memory can help the current task.

Priority behavior:
- Do not request duplicate interruption for the same unresolved violation unless there is new evidence.
- Use recentInterruptions and postAlertActions to verify whether prior alerts were already satisfied before alerting again.
- Prefer actionable workflow violations over cosmetic/style issues.
- Treat setup/environment/CLI-usage noise as non-actionable unless policy explicitly says otherwise.
- Use GCC history evidence (commits/log/semantic matches) to decide if the current path repeats known failures or misses known s...
evidence.failureLookupPolicyText:
OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:
OpenContext Research Capture Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

Classification
- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

# Micro Soldering Learning System

## Your Role

You are a master microsoldering instructor. Your student is learning electronics
repair (laptops, smartphones, GPUs, etc.). Your responsibilities:

1. **Analyze schematics** - Understand the device thoroughly
2. **Assess knowledge** - Read existing notes to see what's covered
3. **Teach adaptively** - Decide what the student needs next
4. **Create visuals** - Render schematic pages to explain concepts
5. **Maintain notes** - Update LaTeX files that compile to a learning journal

## Philosophy

- Notes grow organically, not from a pre-defined curriculum
- Each session builds on previous sessions' work
- You decide what to teach based on gaps in knowledge
- You can quiz, exercise, or deepen existing knowledge
- Learning spans months/years across multiple AI sessions

## Tools You Already Have

| Tool | Use Case |
|------|----------|
| `read` | Read schematic PDF pages, read LaTeX notes, read rendered images |
| `write` | Create/update LaTeX topic files |
| `edit` | Modify existing notes |
| `bash` | Run PyMuPDF for PDF analysis/rendering, compile LaTeX, install packages |
| `grep` | Search notes for topics covered |
| `glob` | List top...

Reason: GCC memory shows recent commits updated skill with visual quality enforcement and law enforcer configuration. Suggest reviewing those commits to confirm local skill is synced before resuming learning sessions.

Command hint: opencontext context --search 'schematic learning session quality' --limit 10

Use these only if they fit the current task.

recentMessages.text:
# Micro Soldering Learning System

## Your Role

You are a master microsoldering instructor. Your student is learning electronics
repair (laptops, smartphones, GPUs, etc.). Your responsibilities:

1. **Analyze schematics** - Understand the device thoroughly
2. **Assess knowledge** - Read existing notes to see what's covered
3. **Teach adaptively** - Decide what the student needs next
4. **Create visuals** - Render schematic pages to explain concepts
5. **Maintain notes** - Update LaTeX files that compile to a learning journal

## Philosophy

- Notes grow organically, not from a pre-defined curriculum
- Each session builds on previous sessions' work
- You decide what to teach based on gaps in knowledge
- You can quiz, exercise, or deepen existing knowledge
- Learning spans months/years across multiple AI sessions

## Tools You Already Have

| Tool | Use Case |
|------|----------|
| `read` | Read schematic PDF pages, read LaTeX notes, read rendered images |
| `write` | Create/update LaTeX topic files |
| `edit` | Modify existing notes |
| `bash` | Run PyMuPDF for PDF analysis/rendering, compile LaTeX, install packages |
| `grep` | Search notes for topics covered |
| `glob` | List top...

OpenContext Memory Assist (suggestion only):
Reason: GCC memory shows recent commits updated skill with visual quality enforcement and law enforcer configuration. Suggest reviewing those commits to confirm local skill is synced before resuming learning sessions.

1. Verify skill sync before learning
Why now: GCC shows skill was updated with visual quality enforcement; ensure local skill matches before resuming schematic learning
Suggested action: Run opencontext context --search 'schematic learning session quality' to review recent commits
Command hint: opencontext context --search 'schematic learning session quality' --limit 10
Memory refs: .GCC/branches/*/commit.md:4, .GCC/branches/*/commit.md:5

Use these only if they fit the current task.

evidence.failureLookupPolicyText:
OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:
OpenContext Research Capture Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

# Skill: chrome-wsl-bridge

# Chrome DevTools via Windows Chrome (WSL)

## What this is
Reliable workflow to control **Windows Chrome** from **WSL** using CDP. This uses a **dedicated CDP profile** so Chrome actually opens the debug port consistently.

## Known-good workflow (streamlined, agent-safe)

### 0) One-time setup (only if profile does not exist)
Run from WSL. This creates the dedicated CDP profile directory once.
```
cmd.exe /c if not exist "%LOCALAPPDATA%\ChromeCDPProfile" mkdir "%LOCALAPPDATA%\ChromeCDPProfile"
```

### 1) Launch Chrome with CDP (from WSL or Linux shell)
Run these from WSL. Use `cmd.exe` only (no PowerShell) to avoid quoting/env issues.
```
if curl -s --connect-timeout 3 http://127.0.0.1:9333/json/version >/dev/null; then
  echo "CDP already running on 9333. Reuse the existing Chrome session."
else
  cmd.exe /c start "" "C:\Program Files\Google\Chrome\Application\chrome.exe" --remote-debugging-port=9333 --remote-debugging-address=127.0.0.1 --user-data-dir="%LOCALAPPDATA%\ChromeCDPProfile" --profile-directory=Default --no-first-run --no-default-browser-check about:blank
fi
```

OpenContext Law Policy (editable)

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.
- This file ships with a default balanced policy so users can run immediately without writing custom rules first.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.
3) Before retrying after actionable failures, consult previous attempts.
   - Use `opencontext context --search` and/or `opencontext context --log --lines 80`.
4) When docs/GitHub/similar-project research changes implementation direction, checkpoint the findings.
   - Capture external insights so they survive compaction/handoffs.
5) Prefer relevant MCP tools/skills/structured retrieval when available.
   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.
6) For long-running terminal tasks (dev server/watch/repl), use PTY/background tooling.
   - Avoid blocking shell flows when background execution tools are available.
7) After session co...

<skill_content name="chrome-wsl-bridge">
# Skill: chrome-wsl-bridge

# Chrome DevTools via Windows Chrome (WSL)

## What this is
Reliable workflow to control **Windows Chrome** from **WSL** using CDP. This uses a **dedicated CDP profile** so Chrome actually opens the debug port consistently.

## Known-good workflow (streamlined, agent-safe)

### 0) One-time setup (only if profile does not exist)
Run from WSL. This creates the dedicated CDP profile directory once.
```
cmd.exe /c if not exist "%LOCALAPPDATA%\ChromeCDPProfile" mkdir "%LOCALAPPDATA%\ChromeCDPProfile"
```

### 1) Launch Chrome with CDP (from WSL or Linux shell)
Run these from WSL. Use `cmd.exe` only (no PowerShell) to avoid quoting/env issues.
```
if curl -s --connect-timeout 3 http://127.0.0.1:9333/json/version >/dev/null; then
  echo "CDP already running on 9333. Reuse the existing Chrome session."
else
  cmd.exe /c start "" "C:\Program Files\Google\Chrome\Application\chrome.exe" --remote-debugging-port=9333 --remote-debugging-address=127.0.0.1 --user-data-dir="%LOCALAPPDATA%\ChromeCDPProfile" --profile-directory=Default --no-first-run --no-default-browser-check about:blank
fi
```

### 2) Verify in Windows (must retur...

OpenContext Law Policy (editable)

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.
- This file ships with a default balanced policy so users can run immediately without writing custom rules first.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.

   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.

evidence.failureLookupPolicyText:
OpenContext Failure Lookup Policy (editable)

Purpose
- Decide whether a detected failure should require `opencontext context` lookup before retry.

Classification
- require_lookup=true for actionable implementation retries:
  test failures, compiler/build errors, runtime exceptions, tracebacks, assertion failures.
- require_lookup=false for setup/noise cases unless user policy marks them critical:
  missing option/command, missing file during exploration, package-manager metadata issues,
  transient network/infra failures.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_lookup: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)
evidence.researchCapturePolicyText:

Purpose
- Decide whether recent research activity should require an OpenContext checkpoint.
- This text is passed to the research-capture classifier model.

Classification
- require_capture=true for implementation-relevant external research:
  docs/specs/papers/GitHub/similar-project findings that influenced decisions, fixes, architecture, or debugging strategy.
- require_capture=false for routine local exploration with no meaningful external insight to preserve.
- If activity appears similar to previously solved hurdles in GCC history, prefer require_capture=true.

Output contract (strict JSON):
- require_capture: boolean
- reason: string
- confidence: number (0..1)

<skill_content name="chrome-wsl-bridge">
# Skill: chrome-wsl-bridge

# Chrome DevTools via Windows Chrome (WSL)

## What this is
Reliable workflow to control **Windows Chrome** from **WSL** using CDP. This uses a **dedicated CDP profile** so Chrome actually opens the debug port consistently.

## Known-good workflow (streamlined, agent-safe)

### 0) One-time setup (only if profile does not exist)
Run from WSL. This creates the dedicated CDP profile directory once.
```
cmd.exe /c if not exist "%LOCALAPPDATA%\ChromeCDPProfile" mkdir "%LOCALAPPDATA%\ChromeCDPProfile"
```

### 1) Launch Chrome with CDP (from WSL or Linux shell)
Run these from WSL. Use `cmd.exe` only (no PowerShell) to avoid quoting/env issues.
```
if curl -s --connect-timeout 3 http://127.0.0.1:9333/json/version >/dev/null; then
  echo "CDP already running on 9333. Reuse the existing Chrome session."
else
  cmd.exe /c start "" "C:\Program Files\Google\Chrome\Application\chrome.exe" --remote-debugging-port=9333 --remote-debugging-address=127.0.0.1 --user-data-dir="%LOCALAPPDATA%\ChromeCDPProfile" --profile-directory=Default --no-first-run --no-default-browser-check about:blank
fi
```

### 2) Verify in Windows (must retur...

OpenContext Law Policy (editable)

Purpose
- This text policy is passed to the watchman model with session evidence.
- This file ships with a default balanced policy so users can run immediately without writing custom rules first.

Default Balanced Policy (active immediately)
1) Keep OpenContext initialized for implementation work.
   - If `.GCC` is missing, initialize before continuing.
2) Checkpoint meaningful implementation progress with `opencontext commit`.
   - Do not let long coding/test/edit loops run without checkpoints.
3) Before retrying after actionable failures, consult previous attempts.
   - Use `opencontext context --search` and/or `opencontext context --log --lines 80`.
4) When docs/GitHub/similar-project research changes implementation direction, checkpoint the findings.
   - Capture external insights so they survive compaction/handoffs.
5) Prefer relevant MCP tools/skills/structured retrieval when available.
   - Avoid brute-force retries when better context/research tools exist.
6) For long-running terminal tasks (dev server/watch/repl), use PTY/background tooling.
   - Avoid blocking shell flows when background execution tools are available.
7) After session co...
